preamble: The lenition or spirantization of a voiceless velar stop /k/ into a velar fricative /x/, a classic example of weakening in the consonant system.

examples.note: Begedkefat spirantization: post-vocalic /k/ → [x] in Biblical Hebrew. Final kaf is always [x].
High German consonant shift of final /k/ to /x/.

Partial Grimm's Law in Iranian: voiceless velar /k/ became velar fricative /x/ before another consonant. E.g., Proto-Indo-Iranian *kšap- 'night' → Avestan xšap-, Old Persian xšāyaθiya 'king'. The cluster *xš is preserved in Ossetian ӕхсин (æxsin) 'lady'.
Kurdish, like other Iranian languages, preserves the Proto-Iranian k→x shift in consonant clusters. E.g., Kurdish xwendin 'to read' shows the fricative reflex of inherited *k in cluster position.

sources: Waterman, J. T. (1966). A History of the German Language.
Windfuhr, G. (2009). The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
allophones_note: /k/ surfaces as [x] in post-vocalic and word-final positions in Biblical Hebrew and High German, while [k] is preserved in word-initial and pre-vocalic positions.
